annotations.label: Butterfly effect

anchor.snippet: the butterfly effect is the sensitive dependence on initial conditions

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of the butterfly effect or sensitive dependence on initial conditions; no matches in Mathlib/ for 'butterfly' or 'sensitive depend'.

annotations.label: Sensitive dependence on initial conditions

anchor.snippet: A dynamical system displays sensitive dependence on initial conditions if points arbitrarily close together separate over time at an exponential rate.

annotations.note: No formalization of sensitive dependence or exponential separation of orbits is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorenz's sensitive dependence (orbit characterization)
annotations.note: Mathlib's Dynamics library covers minimal/transitive maps and topological entropy but not Lorenz-style orbit sensitivity.

annotations.label: Formal sensitive dependence condition

anchor.snippet: If M is the state space for the map

annotations.note: The epsilon-delta sensitivity condition is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Logistic map exhibiting sensitive dependence

anchor.snippet: The simplest mathematical framework exhibiting sensitive dependence on initial conditions is provided by a particular parametrization of the logistic map

annotations.note: Grep for 'logistic' in Mathlib/ returns no results; the logistic map is not defined.

annotations.label: Stretching and folding in the logistic map solution

anchor.snippet: This solution equation clearly demonstrates the two key features of chaos

annotations.note: Neither the logistic map's closed-form solution nor the stretching-and-folding characterization of chaos is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorenz's butterfly effect (Essence of Chaos)

anchor.snippet: The phenomenon that a small alteration in the state of a dynamical system will cause subsequent states to differ greatly from the states that would have followed without the alteration.

annotations.note: No formalization of Lorenz's qualitative definition of the butterfly effect exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lorenz attractor trajectories diverging

anchor.snippet: These figures show two segments of the three-dimensional evolution of two trajectories

annotations.note: Grep for 'Lorenz' in Mathlib/ returns no results; the Lorenz system and its attractor are not formalized.

annotations.label: Recurrence

anchor.snippet: Recurrence , the approximate return of a system toward its initial conditions

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes measure-theoretic (Poincaré) recurrence for conservative systems, which captures the 'approximate return to initial conditions' idea in a more general setting.

annotations.label: Finite predictability from SDIC

anchor.snippet: the presence of SDIC (commonly known as the butterfly effect) implies that chaotic systems have a finite predictability limit

annotations.note: Predictability limits in chaotic systems are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two-week predictability limit

anchor.snippet: the two-week predictability limit was originally estimated based on a doubling time of five days in real-world models

annotations.note: This empirical meteorological estimate is not within Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Third kind of butterfly effect

anchor.snippet: These two distinct mechanisms suggesting finite predictability in the Lorenz 1969 model are collectively referred to as the third kind of butterfly effect.

annotations.note: The Lorenz 1969 model and its associated 'third kind' of butterfly effect are not formalized in Mathlib.
